children.text: One of the most widely discussed features of Caribbean Spanish and a central resource in reggaetón is the variable realization of syllable-final /s/ (e.g.,

).  Historically, this feature traces back to Southern Spain in Seville and Andalusia more broadly, where processes of consonant weakening began as early as the 16th century in lower-class speech before spreading upward through the social hierarchy and outward through colonial expansion. As a result, varieties of Spanish across the Caribbean inherited this feature as part of a broader phonological profile shaped by Andalusian input.
Phonetically, /s/ reduction is often described as a weakening progression of

that occurs in all dialects of spoken Spanish to differing degrees. That is, a fully articulated sibilant [s] may be realized as such (e.g., [los 'mismos]), an aspirated [h] ([loh 'mihmoh]), or, in some cases, deleted altogether ([lo 'mimo]). In Puerto Rico, /s/ reduction falls within Stage 3 of Lipski's weakening pathway, canonically occurring in most cases of pre-consonantal and some pre-vocalic codas, but it does not occur in the onset position. In addition to these three allophones, /s/ can also be realized as a voiced fricative [z] and aspiration [ɦ], as well as a glottal stop [ʔ]  ([loʔ ˈmihmoʔ]). Moreover, speakers use several strategies to distinguish forms rendered homophonous from the lack of /s/ (e.g.,

Because s-reduction is a gradient feature, analysts often use acoustic measures to determine the extent of reduction and to classify tokens allophonically. This typically involves at least a combined Center of Gravity (COG) and Standard Deviation (SD) Measurement in Hertz (Hz). The COG measurement indicates the average frequency of the sound, whereas the SD indicates dispersion of sound relative to that average. That is, a high COG (~6-10kHz) and a low SD are indicative of a high frequency flow of concentrated air moving through a relatively small airway, which occurs with [s]. Contrastively, a lower COG and a higher SD indicate less-concentrated air moving through a larger opening, which occurs with [h]. These differences can also be confirmed visually using a spectrogram, as [s] presents as a high-frequency dark cluster. Below are spectrograms that showcase this variability, including two forms of deletion: one where the etymological place of /s/ is trace-marked with a pause, and another where the segment is elided entirely.

The alternation between these forms is not random, but conditioned by a combination of language-internal and -external factors, including phonological environment, interactional setting, role alignment, and encoded stance and affect. In Puerto Rico, sibilance [s] is contrasted against both [h] and [∅] along axes of differentation relating to notions of formality, education, and institutional authority. Aspiration is then further distinguished from deletion and sibilance along an axis of locality, being positively evaluated as an authentic marker of Puerto Rican identity. Of these variants, deletion is often reported as the most heavily stigmatized, though such evaluations are shown to be influenced in part by the stance and background of the hearer and their perceptions of the speaker's background. Moreover, some research suggests [s] carries gendered and sexuality-based associations, being heard as a "feminine" feature characteristic of the speech of women and gay men.

content.caption: An Indexical Field of /s/ reduction in Puerto Rico

children.text: In musical performance, these variants are not only inherited from the spoken registers of the performers but strategically deployed as part of the stylization of the musical product. S-Reduction in reggaetón, in particular, plays a central role in constructing the voice of the genre, and is adopted by both speakers from within and without Puerto Rico. Its salience is so high that the feature is even visibly marked in online lyric transcriptions through the use of apostrophes (e.g., "

"). The following examples showcase how findings from contemporary research frame lenited /s/ as an enregistered resource for constructing and performing artistic alignment with the broader reggaetón soundscape. Throughout these examples, AVAnnotate's annotation feature provides transcriptions of the lyrics to highlight cases of reduction. For accessibility, this variability is presented binarily as "retention [s] or reduction [h ʔ ø]" marked in brackets as [s] or [-].

S-Reduction is consistently present in reggaetón throughout the genre's history, withstanding globalization and artistic innovation over the years. Listen below to hear how coda /s/ is highly absent in each of the songs, save for some prevocalic tokens. Reductions appear to be the norm in each song, whose release dates range from 1996 to 2022, to showcase the Underground, Luny Tunes, Latin-Pop, and Contemporary eras of reggaetón production.

Consistently, all coda /s/ are susceptible to reduction, though it is consistently preconsonantal /s/ that favors reduction over prevocalic tokens. Additionally, in some studies, etymological /s/ appears more likely to be reduced than when the sibilant marks 2nd-person verb forms or pluralizes nouns (e.g., ¿

?). But overall, s-reduction appears to be more conditioned by stylistic and social factors rather than strictly linguistic structures, which has lead some scholars to frame this variable as an enregistered artistic marker of reggaetón production. That is, it is a central component for distinguishing and situating the reggaetón voice amidst broader popular music soundscapes. Consider the following examples from Ivy Queen: Both were released in 2023, though "Quién Dijo" is written with heavy bachata and (to use the industry term) "Tropical" stylistics, whereas "Bye Bye" is encoded as a pop-infused reggaetón track. Alongside phonation differences in terms of tempo, pitch, and syllabic duration, the rate at which /s/ is retained or reduced also drastically changes.

This is a productive contrast that is identifiable across the music of different artists. It also remains present in collaborations where multiple voices associated with different genres and styles sing together. That is, even within the same composition, reduced /s/ variants delineate sonic, thematic, and poetic space through their association with distinct musical lineages. For example, in "Bella y Sensual", Daddy Yankee remains almost categorically s-less in contrast to the bachata singer Romeo Santos, who is near categorically s-full, despite both vocalists being of Hispano-Caribbean origins.

The enregistered vernacularity of /s/ reduction and its general restriction to reggaetón/urbano music facilitate its use as a way of embodying the reggaetón voice, even if the singer themselves does not reduce /s/ to the same extent outside of reggaetón production. For this reason, use of reduced /s/ by artists of stereotypically s-retaining backgrounds in reggaetón has been a site of particular investigative interest. From this literature, a general tendency emerges. Singers, regardless of sociolinguistic and cultural background, tend to use significantly more reduced variants when performing reggaetón and reggaetón-derived tracks than in other genres of music and speech modalities. Listen below to hear this style shift in Catalonian singer Rosalía and Mexican-American singer Becky G's /s/ production:

This tendency to use more reduction, but restricted to reggaetón, has also been documented in the music of both native and non-native speakers of Spanish, such as Medellinense singers J Balvin and Karol G and the Brazilian crossover artist Anitta. Moreover, the current research on /s/ production suggests that not only is reduction the preferred variant independent of artists' unique sociolinguistic backgrounds, but also that the rate of reduction across performances converges towards a range upwards of 50%. This is highly suggestive that reduced /s/ is now enregistered as a standard phonological element of the reggaetón voice. Importantly, this has been identified as a relatively novel and emergent pattern, first gaining widespread recognizability and conformity in the late 2010s and 2020s.
Below are some figures that trace patterns of s-reduction distributions between singers, genres, and over time to further showcase this convergence toward a standardized singing style.
